6. Classes

6.1. Environment Diagram

  • Note that during the init function we create a function with global frame as enclosing frame.

  • Calling an instance function, e.g. v.magnitude() is effectively the same as Vector2D.magnitude(v) modulo technical details

  • Probably the same for class functions and static functions.

  • Note. enclosing frame for functions is the frame the class was made in. This leads to the following behavior which seems wacky at first:

6.2. Lookup

6.3. Dunder methods

__init__ is effectively an alias for

v = Vector2D() 
v.x = 3
v.y = 4

These are called dunder methods and get invoked impliiclty. For example:

  • print(x) is translated implicitly to print(x.__str__())
  • abs(x) is translated implicitly to x.__abs__()
  • x + y is translated implicitly to x.__add__(y)
  • x - y is translated implicitly to x.__sub__(y)
  • x[y] is translated implicitly to x.__getitem__(y)
  • x[y] = z is translated implicitly to x.__setitem__(y, z)
  • for elt in x is translated implicitly to for elt in x.__iter__()

For a complete list, see here: https://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#special-method-names

Slicing: a[i:j, k:l] roughly becomes a.__getitem__((slice(i, j, None), slice(k, l, None)))

Note: for exception classes: raise A is interpretted as raise A() if A derives from BaseException

6.4. Inheritance

6.5. Class methods and Static methods

class methods have cls atrtibute. Use decorators @classmethod and @staticmethod.

6.6. __repr__ and __str__

The former should output a string of what you would paste in python to create the object

6.7. __future__

from __future__ import annotations let’s you use stuff later in the file
